name: Right to Erasure
summary: Section 43, inserted by the Personal Data Protection (Amendment) Act 2024 (Act A1727), grants data subjects the right to obtain from a data user the erasure of personal data concerning them without undue delay in specified circumstances. This right — sometimes called the 'right to be forgotten' — applies where the personal data is no longer necessary for the purpose for which it was collected, where consent has been withdrawn and no other lawful basis applies, where the data was processed unlawfully, or where erasure is required to comply with a legal obligation. The right is subject to important exceptions including legal claims and statutory obligations.

obligations.obligation: Upon a valid erasure request, erase the personal data without undue delay where one of the grounds for erasure applies
Assess the erasure request against the applicable grounds and exceptions before refusing or complying; document the assessment and the outcome
Where erasure is not possible (e.g., due to a statutory retention obligation), restrict processing of the personal data in the interim and inform the data subject of the reason for non-erasure
Where the data has been shared with third parties (processors or other data users), notify those third parties of the erasure request and require erasure where reasonably practicable
Inform the data subject in writing of the outcome of the erasure request, including the grounds for any partial or full refusal

exceptions.exception: Erasure may be refused where the processing is necessary for compliance with a legal obligation that requires processing under written law
exceptions.basis: Section 43; applicable written laws (e.g., Companies Act, Employment Act, Income Tax Act)
exceptions.conditions: The legal obligation must be specific and applicable to the data in question; a general reference to legal compliance is insufficient; the data user must identify the specific provision requiring retention

exceptions.exception: Erasure may be refused where the personal data is necessary for the establishment, exercise, or defence of legal claims
exceptions.conditions: The legal claim must be real and not merely hypothetical; the data user must have a genuine, active, or reasonably anticipated legal claim in which the data is relevant

exceptions.exception: Erasure may be refused where processing is necessary for public interest purposes, including research, statistics, or archiving in the public interest
exceptions.conditions: The public interest must be genuine; commercial research or archiving for internal business purposes does not qualify as public interest

exceptions.exception: Erasure does not apply to personal data processed in the context of journalistic, academic, artistic, or literary expression where erasure would seriously impair that expression
exceptions.conditions: The expression exception is narrow; it must be weighed against the data subject's fundamental rights; this exception should not be invoked routinely

practicalGuidance: Data users should implement a structured erasure request process that: (1) acknowledges receipt of the request promptly; (2) assesses whether one of the s43 grounds is satisfied; (3) reviews applicable exceptions; (4) if no exception applies, executes secure deletion across all systems (primary, backup, archive) within a defined timeframe; (5) notifies third parties where applicable; (6) responds to the data subject in writing with the outcome. Secure deletion must be technically effective — logical deletion (marking as deleted in a database) that can be reversed does not constitute erasure. Data users should maintain deletion certificates for regulatory purposes. Note that backup tapes and archival systems present a practical challenge — data users should establish a policy for how erasure requests are handled for data that may be included in offline backups, typically by preventing the restoration of the specific record and purging it at the next backup rotation.
commonViolations: Refusing erasure requests without identifying a specific applicable exception
Performing logical deletion (soft delete) and treating this as erasure without ensuring the data cannot be restored
Failing to notify data processors and other recipients of the erasure requirement
Treating erasure requests as access requests and providing data instead of erasing it
Erasing data that is the subject of a compliance or legal hold without first consulting Legal
